text: Hello, This is a unedited full length laparoscopic cholecystectomy video. It is very important that in laparoscopy if you want to learn, you should always try to watch the unedited video. Edited videos are looking very good, very fancy, very attractive but this is not for any educational purposes. Because in edited video, the surgeons generally they cut all their complications and then they put only the correct part. Unedited video will give you the complete task analysis and that way the learning can be better achieved.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis during a laparoscopic cholecystectomy, using a traumatic grasper to hold the gallbladder fundus. Ports are positioned in the epigastric region, midclavicular line below the gallbladder fundus, and laterally. The fundus is pushed towards the right shoulder to facilitate anterior traction on omental adhesions, while a harmonic device is used for adhesiolysis.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device with silicon jaws positioned towards the gallbladder to prevent rupture during adhesiolysis. The duodenum is visibly adhered to the neck of the gallbladder, and the surgeon employs a stripping technique to separate the adhesion bands, pulling them down to free the duodenum.

The surgeon identifies Rovier's sulcus, a groove separating the right lobe of the liver from the caudate lobe at the level of the porta hepatis. The surgical team ensures that window formation and tissue separation occur above this sulcus to avoid damaging the common bile duct.

The surgeon performs peritoneal dissection at the attachment site of the posterior peritoneum with the body of the gallbladder, crucial for proper dissection of the cystic pedicle and achieving a clear view of the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon uses the tip of the harmonic device to knock the peritoneum, facilitating the formation of a blade and opening hydrogen bonds. This technique helps in automatically separating the posterior peritoneum from the liver at the level of the gallbladder body, ensuring the dissection remains above Rovier's sulcus.

The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the Hartmann's pouch while dissecting the posterior peritoneum. Subsequently, anterolateral traction is applied to the anterior peritoneum, using the tip of the harmonic device to knock the peritoneum, facilitating the formation of a dissection plane as the peritoneum separates.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device to create a posterior window by turning its tip with the convex edge towards the liver, lifting it to separate the artery from the liver tissue.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to create and enlarge an anterior window near the liver, positioning the convex edge of the instrument towards the liver for precise manipulation.

The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to perform anterior dissection, separating the cystic duct from the cystic artery. The tip of the Maryland is positioned between these structures, gradually opening the jaw to create a 22-millimeter space, sufficient for applying three clips to the cystic artery.

The surgeon uses the Maryland dissector to grasp and lift the cystic duct, allowing visualization of the duct, common bile duct (CBD), artery, and the anterior and posterior windows against the liver surface.

The surgeon applies titanium clips to the cystic artery using a clip applicator. Three clips are placed with precise spacing: three millimeters between the first and second clip, and six millimeters between the second and third clip. After securing the clips, the artery is cut using scissors, ensuring both limbs of the scissors are visible during the incision.

The surgeon applies Mishra's knot to the cystic duct, using a 20-centimeter suture. The Hartsman pouch grasper holds the loop to prevent shearing, while the knot is tied extracorporeally with a sequence of one hitch, three winds, and three locks. This knot secures the duct without risk of cat eye stone formation, ensuring a tight closure.

The surgeon applies an extracorporeal knot to the cystic duct, ensuring a secure closure with a dumbbell effect. A clip is placed above the knot to prevent bile leakage when the duct is cut. The harmonic scalpel is then used to separate the cystic duct.

The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the liver using anteromedial and anterolateral traction. The neck of the gallbladder is pushed anteriorly and medially towards the falciform ligament, followed by lateral traction. The dissection maintains a clear areolar plane to prevent rupture of the gallbladder and minimize bleeding from the liver.

The surgeon uses the left hand to adjust the grasper holding the gallbladder, providing traction to separate it from the liver. The dissection proceeds along a clear areolar plane, allowing separation without energy devices. Various instruments like a hook, spatula, or scissors with monopolar may be used, but the left hand's traction is crucial for maintaining the dissection plane.

The surgeon adjusts the 30-degree telescope to improve visualization of the gallbladder fossa, removing the fundus grasper as it is no longer necessary. The left hand provides traction while the surgeon separates the remaining fundus of the gallbladder from the liver. The surgeon examines the Calot's fossa, liver surface, and checks for any bile leaks or open ducts. Minimal harmonic mode is used to control any oozing from the liver. Finally, the last part of the gallbladder is detached from the liver, completing the cholecystectomy.

The surgeon inserts a 10 mm claw forceps through the epigastric port to grasp the neck of the gallbladder at the site where the clip was applied over the cystic duct. The cystic duct is concealed within the cannula, which is then withdrawn, allowing for the extraction of the gallbladder.
